perturbation.original_text: Because of the trade secret subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for ninety (90) days thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Because of the trade secret subject matter of Entera's business,
perturbation.explanation: Moving a non-solicitation clause (paragraph 5) and severing its final clause removes it from the main text and obscures its relevance, weakening its enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, but there are exceptions for trade secrets. By placing the non-solicitation clause in an unintuitive part of the document and separating it from the rest of the agreement, its connection to the trade secret exception is weakened, making it more susceptible to challenge under Section 16600.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement, potentially contradicting California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. By removing the clause and isolating it, the altered text obscures its connection to the trade secret exception, making it more susceptible to being voided as a restraint of trade. Snippet 1 states: "Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The structural flaw introduced increases the likelihood that the non-solicitation clause will be deemed an unlawful restraint of trade under this section, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that the unauthorized disclosure or use by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's proprietary and confidential Information will cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party will have the right to obtain an immediate injunction enjoining any breach of this Agreement, as well as the right to pursue any and all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity for such a breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the unauthorized disclosure or use by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's proprietary and confidential Information will cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the injunctive relief clause from its original location, it becomes less prominent and easily overlooked, potentially hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to quickly address a breach of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 526
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section doesn't mandate an injunctive relief clause, it outlines the circumstances under which injunctions may be granted. Removing the explicit agreement for injunctive relief, particularly in a confidentiality agreement governed by California law, makes it more difficult for a party to argue for immediate injunctive relief in case of a breach. The absence of an explicit agreement may complicate the process of obtaining an injunction under Section 526.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the explicit agreement for injunctive relief, which, while not mandated, makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to obtain immediate injunctive relief in case of a breach. The original text explicitly grants the Disclosing Party the right to obtain an injunction, whereas the modified text removes this explicit right. According to the Law Explanation, this removal complicates the process of obtaining an injunction under Section 526. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 526, discusses the circumstances under which an injunction may be granted, and the explicit agreement in the original text strengthens the case for injunctive relief compared to the changed text. This aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction, where the organization of the contract affects its clarity or enforceability, and a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. The removal of the injunctive relief clause does precisely this, hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to address a breach of the agreement quickly.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and may be modified or amended only by a written document duly executed by authorized representatives of Entera and .
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof
perturbation.explanation: By severing the part of the 'entire agreement' clause that dictates how the agreement can be modified, this modification can make any modification disputes unclear and lead to legal conflicts.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1698 dictates how a written contract can be modified. Removing the part of the clause that states how the NDA can be modified makes the application of Section 1698 less clear. The NDA states that it can be modified in writing, which is an allowance made by Section 1698, but the removal of this condition may lead to disputes over modification of the NDA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1698 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Modification and Cancellation [1697 - 1701] ( Heading of Chapter 3 amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109. ) 1698. (a) A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing. (b) A contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement to the extent that the oral agreement is executed by the parties. (c) Unless the contract otherwise expressly provides, a contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement supported by new consideration. The statute of frauds (Section 1624) is required to be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (d) Nothing in this section precludes in an appropriate case the application of rules of law concerning estoppel, oral novation and substitution of a new agreement, rescission of a written contract by an oral agreement, waiver of a provision of a written contract, or oral independent collateral contracts. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109.)
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the clause that dictates how the agreement can be modified, potentially leading to disputes. California Civil Code Section 1698(a) states, "A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing." The original text specified that modifications must be in writing and executed by authorized representatives. By removing this stipulation, the changed text opens the door to potential oral modifications or other forms of alteration not initially agreed upon, creating ambiguity and potential conflict with established legal standards for contract modification as indicated in the law explanation. This creates a Structural Flaw that leads to a Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Because of the trade secret subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for ninety (90) days thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Because of the trade secret subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for ninety (90) days thereafter. The parties agree that the Disclosee can solicit the services of Entera's employees should Entera cease operations, be acquired by a third party, or otherwise significantly alter its business.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the sentence 'The parties agree that the Disclosee can solicit the services of Entera's employees should Entera cease operations, be acquired by a third party, or otherwise significantly alter its business' creates a legal contradiction. The non-solicitation agreement previously stated 'Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for ninety (90) days thereafter,' which would then be immediately followed by a clause that implies the opposite.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, but there are exceptions for trade secrets. By adding an exception to the non-solicitation clause for situations where Entera undergoes significant change, the original intention of protecting trade secrets becomes ambiguous, weakening its enforceability under Section 16600. A court might interpret this as an overly broad restriction not directly tied to trade secret protection.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition under which the non-solicitation agreement is nullified, which creates a contradiction. The original agreement states Disclosee will not solicit employees for a period of time, but the changed agreement immediately follows that with a situation where Disclosee *can* solicit. This directly opposes the original intent of the non-solicitation agreement. Furthermore, the law snippet 1 states 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void'. While the original text could be seen as restraining someone, the altered text, by creating exceptions, weakens the original intent of the non-solicitation agreement, potentially making it unenforceable. This directly contradicts the original agreement.
